title: Anna Karenina
author: Leo Tolstoy
note: Masterclass on self-deception. Propulsive despite the length.
translator: No strong consensus — Pevear/Volokhonsky or Maude.

title: The Death of Ivan Ilyich
author: Leo Tolstoy
note: Short novella on dying and what mattered. Devastating.
translator: No strong consensus — Pevear/Volokhonsky or Maude.

title: Chekhov's Selected Stories
author: Anton Chekhov
note: The short story as he essentially invented it. Quiet, devastating.
translator: No strong consensus — Constance Garnett or Pevear/Volokhonsky.

title: Fathers and Sons
author: Ivan Turgenev
note: Generational conflict; shorter and more accessible than the giants.

title: Notes from Underground
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
note: Short (~150pp), intense, foundational. The more demanding option.

title: Kokoro
author: Natsume Soseki
note: Formal restraint, friendship and shame, generational change.

title: Snow Country
author: Yasunari Kawabata
note: Atmospheric, slim, geisha and ennui.

title: The Makioka Sisters
author: Junichiro Tanizaki
note: Long family saga, Osaka before WWII.

title: The Sailor Who Fell from Grace with the Sea
author: Yukio Mishima
note: Controlled, disturbing, short.

title: The Woman in the Dunes
author: Kobo Abe
note: Surreal/existentialist; nightmare with sand. Fills non-realist gap.

title: One Hundred Years of Solitude
author: Gabriel Garcia Marquez
note: The anchor. Family curse, mythic time, the Buendías.
translator: Gregory Rabassa (García Márquez said better than the original).

title: Ficciones
author: Jorge Luis Borges
note: Short stories. Labyrinths, mirrors, infinite libraries. Gateway.
translator: No strong consensus — Kerrigan (Grove Ficciones) or Hurley (Penguin Collected Fictions).

title: Aunt Julia and the Scriptwriter
author: Mario Vargas Llosa
note: Funny, narratively driven, autobiographical.

title: Pedro Páramo
author: Juan Rulfo
note: Very short (~120pp), foundational. Direct influence on García Márquez.
translator: Margaret Sayers Peden long the standard; Douglas J. Weatherford (2023) also well-received.

title: By Night in Chile
author: Roberto Bolano
note: Short, hallucinatory. Post-boom but adjacent.

title: Madame Bovary
author: Gustave Flaubert
note: The masterwork on self-deception. Built for you.
translator: No strong consensus — Lydia Davis (prestige modern), Steegmuller, or Geoffrey Wall.

title: The Red and the Black
author: Stendhal
note: Ambition and self-deception in post-Napoleonic France.

title: Middlemarch
author: George Eliot
note: The bigger English anchor — but it eats the quarter if you commit.

title: Pere Goriot
author: Honoré de Balzac
note: Paris, ambition, gateway to the Comédie humaine.

title: Washington Square
author: Henry James
note: Short, sharp. Continuous with Wharton's tradition.

title: Bewilderment
author: Richard Powers
note: Father-son, neuroscience and grief; a tighter, slimmer Powers than the doorstops.

title: Lila
author: Marilynne Robinson
note: Quiet Calvinist interiority; Gilead from the outside in.

title: The Buried Giant
author: Kazuo Ishiguro
note: Post-Arthurian fog of memory; Ishiguro pushed into allegory.
